figures.caption: Table 1: Inputs and operators used in the LE-DM reconstruction. The same formulation is used for stationary and moving boundaries; only the signed-distance field and prescribed boundary velocity change.

Figure 1: LE-DM processing pipeline for one time slab. The Lagrangian tracks and instantaneous body state provide the inputs. The signed-distance field defines the fluid, shell, and solid-interior node sets; the track distribution defines the interpolation, measurement weights, and coverage-adaptive smoothness; and the body kinematics define the shell and solid-interior targets. These quantities are assembled into the single saddle-point system of Eq. 20. The output is an Eulerian velocity field on the fixed grid, with discrete incompressibility imposed in the open fluid and rigid-body kinematics imposed at the boundary shell.
Table 2: Role of the three assessment cases used to evaluate the LE-DM reconstruction.

Figure 2: Velocity-field comparison on the meridional slice through the sphere center at the snapshot of peak sphere velocity, ωt = 0. Top row: Wo = 2; bottom row: Wo = 3. Columns, left to right: analytical solution, base CCM reconstruction, LE-DM reconstruction, and velocity magnitude normalized by U0 versus polar angle θ on a ring of radius a+ 2∆. The angular profiles compare the analytical solution, the base CCM reconstruction, and LE-DM. The contour columns share a common color scale. The dashed circle marks the Stokes-layer thickness δBL. In the analytical and LE-DM panels, the gray disc marks the sphere mask. In the base CCM reconstruction, the sphere interior is shown as reconstructed because no solid mask is supplied.

Figure 3: Resolution and near-interface sensitivity for the oscillating-sphere analytical assessment. (a) Relative RMS error versus ∆/ℓp for the bulk region and wall band at Wo = 3; the dashed vertical line marks ∆ = ℓp. (b) First-cell error ε1stcell/U0 for the base CCM reconstruction and LE-DM at Wo = 2 and Wo = 3. (c) Ring-profile RMS deviation on the φ = 2∆ sampling ring for the same two reconstructions. The comparison is diagnostic and is intended to show where the dynamic mask changes the near-interface solution.
Table 4: Diagnostic comparison between the base CCM reconstruction and LE-DM for the oscillating-sphere case.

Figure 4: Reconstruction error versus tracer density for the CFD rising-sphere validation case. (a) Full-domain relative RMS error εF/U0, (b) wake-region error εwake/U0, and (c) wall-region error εwall/U0, each plotted against ∆/ℓp for grid spacing ∆ = 1.0 mm (circles), 1.5 mm (triangles), and 2.0 mm (squares). Errors are averaged over the final five snapshots of each run. The wall-region error saturates at low ∆/ℓp, while the wake error continues to improve with increasing tracer density, reflecting the stronger velocity gradients concentrated in the near-wake.
Table 5: Validation metrics for reconstruction of the CFD simulated field.

Figure 5: Reconstructed velocity field for the CFD synthetic-track validation case at the lowest tracer density, Np = 3× 105. (a) Velocity magnitude on the x–y and z–y meridional slices through the sphere center, comparing the CFD reference field with the LE-DM reconstruction at grid spacing ∆ = 1.0, 1.5, and 2.0 mm on a common color scale. (b) Velocity magnitude versus polar angle θ sampled around the wake for the same two planes, comparing the CFD reference with LE-DM at the three grid spacings. (c) Velocity magnitude along the rise axis through the wake centerline. The reconstruction tracks the CFD reference closely through the bulk wake; residual differences concentrate in the steep-gradient region next to the sphere, where at the finest grid the ∆/2 = 0.5 mm shell band approaches the resolution of the CFD reference itself, consistent with the resolution and tracer-density trends in Table 5.

Figure 6: Experimental facility. (a) Side view and top view of the octagonal acrylic tank (110 mm side length, 700 mm height) used for the buoyancy-driven sphere experiment, showing the laser illumination volume, sphere release mechanism, and four Phantom v2640 high-speed cameras. (b) Photograph of the assembled tomographic PTV setup. Adapted from Jose et al. (2026)

Figure 7: Experimental demonstration of LE-DM on a freely rising rigid sphere (d = 11.11 mm, refractiveindex-matched solution), at the snapshot ys = 6.9 mm, |Us| = 0.264 m/s. (a) Velocity magnitude on a meridional slice through the sphere center, for the base CCM reconstruction, LE-DM, VIC# (Jeon et al., 2022), and a direct binning of the tracks, all on a common grid and shared color scale, shown without spatial smoothing. Only LE-DM masks the sphere interior (gray disc). The base CCM reconstruction and VIC# place reconstructed velocity inside the body region, as neither carries a body model, and binning leaves roughly 58% of fluid cells empty (light gray) because naive interpolation cannot close the field where tracks are absent. The white circle marks the detected sphere surface. (b) Node classification produced by the signed-distance function on the same slice, showing solid-interior nodes (set to the body velocity), boundary-shell nodes (where the no-slip condition is enforced), and open-fluid nodes (reconstructed). (c) Mean reconstructed speed over the shell band versus time for all four methods, with the independently measured sphere speed |Us(t)| as a dashed reference. LE-DM follows |Us(t)| by construction, since its shell nodes are constrained to the body velocity, while the base CCM reconstruction, VIC#, and binning lie below it at every snapshot, quantifying how far an unconstrained field departs from the independently measured surface speed.

Table 6: Cross-method comparison on the experimental rising-sphere snapshot (ys = 6.9 mm, |Us| = 0.264 m/s). Shell slip is the mean ∥u−Us∥ over the enforced Dirichlet band (0 < φ < 0.5 mm). Near-wall fluid slip is the mean over the open-fluid band, normalized by |Us|. Quiescent RMS is the root-mean-square speed in the far-field region. Wake mean and wake 95th percentile are taken over the wake region. Notrack fraction is the fraction of wake cells without an instantaneous track within one grid spacing, and the empty-node fraction is the fraction of fluid cells left unfilled by binning after window growth. The raw PTV column reports the corresponding statistic of the scattered track velocities for reference, and binning is a single-snapshot inverse-distance-weighted ensemble average on the same grid. All fields are sampled on a common grid.
